title: Performance & View Transitions
description: Zero-JS by default, CSP nonces, and animated partial swaps

blocks.text: Pages that don't render an island, a <Partial>, an f-client-nav boundary, or an f-view-transition body ship 0 KB of JavaScript. The bootloader <script> tag and the Link: …; rel="modulepreload" response header are both skipped automatically — no flag, no opt-in.
The renderer tracks a needsClientRuntime flag during render. If anything on the page needs the runtime, the bootloader and preload header are wired in. If not, they're omitted entirely.

rows: Receives DOM swaps from partial nav
Ships the AOT navigation chunk + manifest

blocks.text: Routing's __ (AOT) and ___ (SSG) prefixes opt pages into client-side navigation and/or build-time prerendering. The performance shape:
AOT chunks are hash-isolated under /_howl/js/{BUILD_ID}/ and served with Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable in production. BUILD_ID rotates each deploy so the URL is the cache key.

SSG is a render-time optimisation, not a build-time one — your build still has to JSON-stringify each prerendered HTML into the snapshot. For 10k+ dynamic pages, prefer dynamic SSR with CDN edge caching over SSG.

The csp() middleware generates a fresh per-request nonce, propagates it to the bootloader <script nonce="…">, and emits script-src 'nonce-…' 'strict-dynamic' 'self'. Strict CSP works with no 'unsafe-inline' required.

Add f-view-transition to your <body> and partial swaps will be wrapped in document.startViewTransition() — animate between DOM states with regular CSS. The runtime falls back to a direct DOM swap on browsers that don't implement the API. Both SSR partial navs and AOT chunk swaps honor the flag; the post-nav scroll is also deferred until the transition finishes so animations don't fight a mid-flight scroll-to-top.
Pair f-view-transition with f-client-nav. Without f-client-nav, browser-native page navigation will tear down the document and the transition won't run.
